Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd just end up scrolling through my phone or watching something unrelated. When I did study, it was usually late at night when everything else quieted down. I have about 18 hours a week supposedly dedicated to studying, but a lot of that time gets eaten up by just staring at materials without really absorbing them. Being 28 and still figuring this out makes me feel like I should have it together by now, but I don't.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd have good intentions at the start of each week, but then life would get in the way—work stuff, family obligations, just general exhaustion. Deadlines were really the only thing that forced me into any kind of consistent study mode. Without that external pressure, I'd drift and procrastinate. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything until the last possible moment.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about something due soon or just this vague guilt that I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I ended up not actually doing much. The anxiety of falling behind would push me to at least open the platform. Other times, I'd see peers posting in discussions and feel like I should show my face too.
Q4: There were brief periods where I got into a rhythm, especially early in the semester when my motivation was higher. I'd check the LMS in the morning with my coffee, almost like checking social media. But that habit never really stuck for long. Once I started feeling bored or overwhelmed with the 14 courses I was juggling, logging in felt more like a chore than a routine. The CBT sessions I did helped me recognize these patterns, but changing them is another story.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing things out. I appreciated when instructors posted detailed notes or transcripts alongside videos. Discussion posts were also helpful because seeing how others interpreted the material gave me different perspectives. The videos were okay, but I often found myself zoning out during them unless I was actively taking notes.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the supplementary materials and optional readings. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize, and those extras just didn't make the cut. Some of the interactive activities felt tedious and not worth the effort when I was already struggling to complete the basics. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The peer interaction features I used somewhat, but not as much as I probably should have.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. If something was going to help me pass, I'd engage with it. I also valued things that were clearly explained and didn't require me to dig through layers of confusing instructions. When I could finish something and feel like I actually learned or accomplished something concrete, that made it worthwhile. Otherwise, it just felt like busywork adding to my stress.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they existed in a different universe. Required stuff had deadlines and consequences, so I'd force myself through them even when bored. Optional things? They'd sit there taunting me while I convinced myself I'd do them 'when I had time.' The pressure wasn't there, so my already low motivation couldn't carry me through. It's frustrating because I know some of those optional materials could have helped me understand things better.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download PDFs and readings to my phone or laptop and study them offline, especially when I didn't have reliable internet or was commuting. I also used external YouTube videos and websites when the course materials didn't click for me. A lot of my actual learning happened with a notebook and pen, just writing things out repeatedly. That kind of studying wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing printed notes or re-reading downloaded materials for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd work through practice problems on paper, talk through concepts with a friend over coffee, or even just think through ideas while doing other tasks. My best understanding often came from these quiet, offline moments. The system only sees clicks and timestamps, not the actual cognitive work happening in my head.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a lecture video or reading once, download what I needed, and then work with it offline for days. My internet isn't always reliable, and streaming videos eats up data I can't really afford to waste. So one access could represent hours of actual study time spread across multiple days. The LMS would just show that single login though, making it look like I barely engaged.
Q12: There were definitely low points, usually when multiple deadlines piled up across my 14 courses. My anxiety would spike, and instead of tackling things, I'd freeze up and avoid everything. Feeling bored with the material didn't help either—it made every task feel like pushing through mud. Financial stress and just general life exhaustion as a 28-year-old trying to make this work also pulled my attention away. Those were the times I'd log in but not really do anything meaningful.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with personal stuff, the LMS became something I'd just skim through mindlessly. Confusion about assignment requirements made me avoid starting things because I didn't know where to begin. The cognitive overload of managing so many courses meant I'd sometimes just stare at the screen without processing anything. My low resilience meant setbacks hit me harder than they probably should have.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing in the background while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through readings without any of it actually registering. The boredom would set in, and I'd be physically present in the LMS but mentally checked out. It's like my body was going through the motions while my brain had already given up for the day.
Q15: Constantly. I'd know an assignment was important, see it sitting there, and still avoid it. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like if I didn't start, I couldn't fail at it. Boring topics were the worst because I couldn't muster any interest to push through. I'd tell myself I'd feel more motivated tomorrow, but tomorrow would come with the same dread. The CBT sessions helped me understand this avoidance pattern, but breaking it is still a work in progress.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. As a deadline approached, my LMS activity would spike dramatically—suddenly I'd be watching all the videos, reading everything, posting in discussions. It was stressful and not a healthy way to learn, but it was the only way I could make myself engage. The panic of a deadline was stronger than my boredom or anxiety.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got positive feedback, it gave me a brief boost and made me want to engage more. But negative or critical feedback would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. My high anxiety meant I'd sometimes not even check feedback because I was scared of what it might say. The timing mattered too—feedback that came too late felt pointless since I'd already moved on mentally.
Q18: The course structure had a big impact. When things were laid out clearly with obvious pathways, I could navigate even when unmotivated. But when the structure was confusing or required me to figure out what to do next, I'd get overwhelmed and disengage. Having 14 courses meant I needed things to be straightforward—I didn't have mental energy to decode complicated layouts. Structured deadlines helped, but too much flexibility left me drifting.
Q19: Clear instructions and organized modules made engagement easier. When I could see exactly what was expected and by when, I could at least attempt to plan. What made it harder was when materials were scattered across different sections or when expectations were vague. Also, courses that relied heavily on video content were tough for me since I'm more of a read-write learner. I needed text-based alternatives that weren't always available.
Q20: That sounds familiar. I think sometimes you're in the LMS trying to figure things out, watching videos, reading materials, genuinely attempting to engage—but then you hit a wall. Maybe the assignment feels too overwhelming, or you don't understand what's being asked, or anxiety kicks in. So all that activity doesn't translate into submissions. You're present but paralyzed. The intention is there, but execution fails.
Q21: That could definitely be me at times. I'd do a lot of learning offline—reading downloaded materials, writing notes, discussing with people outside the platform. My exam score was decent enough to pass, even though my assignment completion was low. Some people just don't need to be constantly clicking around the LMS to absorb information. The platform captures activity, not actual learning or understanding.
Q22: Course logs can show when you were active, how often you logged in, what you clicked on. That's useful for seeing patterns of presence. But they miss everything that happens offline, the quality of attention during online time, and the emotional state behind the activity. Someone could have high engagement numbers while being completely checked out mentally. The logs show behavior, not learning or genuine engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being present and invested. For me, real engagement is rare because boredom and anxiety get in the way. When I am truly engaged, I'm asking questions, making connections, wanting to know more. But honestly, that state is hard to reach and harder to maintain.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I struggled with anxiety before even opening it, or how boredom made every task feel pointless. It can't capture the offline studying, the conversations with friends about course material, or the mental processing that happened while I was doing other things. It also can't show that I'm a 28-year-old with limited resources trying to juggle way too many courses while dealing with high stress. The human context behind the data is completely invisible.
